original_hypothesis.title: ERC20Airdrop2 Permanent Fund Lock if withdrawalWindow Initialized to Zero
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ERC20Airdrop2.init does not validate that _withdrawalWindow > 0. If initialized with _withdrawalWindow = 0, the getBalance function divides by withdrawalWindow, causing a division-by-zero revert. All calls to withdraw() would permanently revert, locking all claimed airdrop tokens forever.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The deployment script doesn't show ERC20Airdrop2 deployment, so the initialization parameters are unknown. The contract's init function accepts _withdrawalWindow as uint64 with no zero check. getBalance computes: `balance * (...) / withdrawalWindow` which reverts if withdrawalWindow == 0.
deployment_assumptions: withdrawalWindow is set to 0 during initialization
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment evidence for ERC20Airdrop2 initialization found in provided scripts; init function at ERC20Airdrop2.sol lines 56-71 has no validation for _withdrawalWindow > 0

proof_of_signal.pattern: No validation _withdrawalWindow > 0 in init; getBalance divides by withdrawalWindow

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: timeBasedAllowance = balance * (...) / withdrawalWindow — division by zero when withdrawalWindow == 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Incorrect initialization permanently locks all claimed tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All airdrop tokens become permanently unrecoverable

economic_questions: What is the planned withdrawalWindow value for deployment?
Can the owner recover from this by changing config?
Is there a rescue mechanism for locked funds?
validation_requirements: Confirm getBalance reverts on division by zero
Check if setConfig can fix the issue (setConfig only changes claim params, not withdrawalWindow)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of airdrop for all users

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm no setter exists for withdrawalWindow post-init

validation_result.impact_summary: If ERC20Airdrop2 is initialized with withdrawalWindow=0, all withdraw() calls permanently revert due to division by zero in getBalance(), locking claimed tokens forever with no setter to fix it post-deployment.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ERC20Airdrop2.init function (lines 56-71) accepts _withdrawalWindow as uint64 with no validation that it is greater than zero. The getBalance function (lines 95-118) computes `balance * (block.timestamp.min(claimEnd + withdrawalWindow) - claimEnd) / withdrawalWindow`. When withdrawalWindow is 0: (1) `claimEnd + 0 = claimEnd`, (2) `min(block.timestamp, claimEnd) - claimEnd = 0` when block.timestamp >= claimEnd, (3) `balance * 0 / 0` causes a Panic(0x12) revert in Solidity 0.8.x since division by zero reverts even when the numerator is zero. Furthermore, the `ongoingWithdrawals` modifier requires `claimEnd <= block.timestamp <= claimEnd + withdrawalWindow`, which with withdrawalWindow=0 reduces to `block.timestamp == claimEnd` exactly—a single-second window. Even if hit, getBalance still reverts. There is no setter for withdrawalWindow post-initialization, and MerkleClaimable.setConfig only updates claimStart, claimEnd, and merkleRoot. The only remediation would be a UUPS proxy upgrade.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 56-71 of ERC20Airdrop2.sol confirm no zero check on _withdrawalWindow. Lines 111-118 confirm division by withdrawalWindow. Solidity 0.8.24 arithmetic reverts on division by zero. MerkleClaimable.setConfig (lines 56-65 of MerkleClaimable.sol) confirms no withdrawalWindow setter exists.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No validation that _withdrawalWindow > 0. Setting it to 0 causes permanent division-by-zero in getBalance(), and there is no post-init setter for this field.
Division by withdrawalWindow. When withdrawalWindow == 0, even though the numerator evaluates to 0 (0 * balance = 0), Solidity 0.8.x reverts on 0/0 with Panic(0x12).

calling_sequence: 2. ongoingWithdrawals modifier checks: claimEnd <= block.timestamp <= claimEnd + withdrawalWindow. With withdrawalWindow=0, this requires block.timestamp == claimEnd exactly.
3. Even if the modifier passes (exact timestamp match), getBalance(user) is called
5. This evaluates to balance * 0 / 0, which reverts with Panic(0x12) in Solidity 0.8.x
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: withdraw() -> ongoingWithdrawals modifier (line 48-51, nearly impossible to pass when withdrawalWindow=0) -> getBalance(user) (line 95-118) -> division by zero at line 113-114. Even if a user manages to call at the exact block.timestamp == claimEnd, the division by zero in getBalance causes revert. There is no alternative withdrawal path and no setter for withdrawalWindow.

deployment_assumptions_checked: withdrawalWindow is set to 0 during initialization
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts for ERC20Airdrop2 are provided. DeployERC20Airdrop.s.sol deploys ERC20Airdrop (a different contract), not ERC20Airdrop2. No evidence of intended withdrawalWindow value exists.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is a misconfiguration vulnerability rather than an active attack. If the deployer or initialization transaction sets _withdrawalWindow to 0 (either intentionally by mistake, or via a compromised/malicious deployer), all claimed airdrop tokens become permanently locked. Users can claim tokens (claimedAmount increases) but can never withdraw them. The ongoingWithdrawals modifier creates an effectively impossible window (single block at exact claimEnd timestamp), and even if bypassed, getBalance reverts with division by zero. Since there is no setter for withdrawalWindow and MerkleClaimable.setConfig only modifies claimStart/claimEnd/merkleRoot, the only fix would be a UUPS proxy upgrade.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If deployed with withdrawalWindow=0, the entire airdrop allocation becomes permanently unrecoverable through normal contract operations. All users who claim tokens will have their claimedAmount recorded but will never be able to withdraw. This constitutes a complete loss of the airdrop for all participants. The contract is UUPS-upgradeable, so the owner could deploy a new implementation to fix the issue, but this requires significant effort and governance action.
recommended_mitigations: Consider adding a setter function for withdrawalWindow (owner-only) as a recovery mechanism
Add the same validation in a constructor or separate validation function
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ERC20Airdrop2 via UUPS proxy with withdrawalWindow=0, valid claimStart/claimEnd/merkleRoot, and a funded vault. 2. Claim tokens for a valid user during the claim window. 3. After claimEnd, attempt to call withdraw(user). 4. Observe revert due to division by zero (or WITHDRAWALS_NOT_ONGOING if not at exact claimEnd timestamp). 5. Verify no setter exists for withdrawalWindow.
uncertainties: The actual deployment parameters for ERC20Airdrop2 are unknown since no deployment script exists for it
UUPS upgradeability provides a theoretical recovery path, but requires governance action
additional_testing_needed: Verify that Solidity 0.8.24 indeed reverts on 0/0 division (standard behavior, but worth confirming)
Test the ongoingWithdrawals modifier boundary conditions with withdrawalWindow=0
Confirm that no other code path can modify withdrawalWindow after initialization
